meta.title: SIG-AE-1: Semantic Modeling, Writeback, and Governed Delivery

domains.name: Semantic Layer Design and Input Tables

questions.stem: Why do Sigma teams publish reusable base datasets?

options.text: To create a governed semantic layer that multiple workbooks can reuse
options.explanation: Reusable datasets centralize business logic and metric definitions so multiple workbooks share a single governed source, ensuring consistent numbers across the organization.
The purpose of published datasets is to enable safe self-service by providing a trusted starting point, not to prevent it.
Published datasets still generate warehouse queries when workbooks consume them; they manage and govern query patterns rather than remove them.

options.text: To replace every workbook with CSV exports
options.explanation: Published datasets are live, queryable objects in Sigma; they do not convert workbook results into static CSV exports.

questions.stem: What is the main difference between a base dataset and a derived dataset in Sigma?

options.text: A derived dataset builds on another dataset's logic, while a base dataset starts closer to the source
options.explanation: A base dataset connects closely to source tables and defines foundational logic, while a derived dataset inherits and extends that logic—adding additional joins, calculations, or filters—without rewriting the upstream definitions.

options.text: A base dataset can never be shared
options.explanation: Base datasets are specifically designed to be shared and reused as governed sources across the workspace.

options.text: A derived dataset cannot contain calculations
options.explanation: Derived datasets can include their own calculations on top of inherited columns; extending calculations is one of their primary use cases.
The distinction is functionally meaningful: lineage, impact analysis, and update propagation behave differently depending on whether a dataset is base or derived.

questions.stem: Why document columns with descriptions and tags in Sigma?

options.text: To help users understand definitions and find trusted fields more easily
options.explanation: Column descriptions explain what a field measures and how it is calculated, while tags enable users to search and filter the data catalog, both of which reduce misuse of fields with similar-sounding names.

options.text: To convert workbooks into templates automatically
options.explanation: Column-level documentation metadata does not trigger any template creation workflow; templates are managed separately.
Column descriptions are metadata annotations; they have no effect on workspace or connection permission rules.
Export capabilities are controlled by workbook permissions and workspace settings, not by whether columns have descriptions.

questions.stem: A semantic layer should balance explorer flexibility with governance. Which design choice best supports that balance?

options.text: Publish trusted datasets for reuse while still allowing derived analysis in downstream workbooks
options.explanation: Published datasets encode approved business logic as a stable foundation, while still permitting downstream workbook authors to add their own dimensions, filters, and calculations, which preserves both consistency and exploration flexibility.

options.text: Prevent all analysts from creating new calculations
options.explanation: Blocking all new calculations prevents the exploratory analysis that makes self-service analytics valuable; analysts would be unable to answer questions that the semantic layer did not anticipate.

options.text: Force every workbook to query raw tables directly
options.explanation: Forcing every workbook to query raw tables bypasses the semantic layer entirely, allowing each author to define metrics differently and undermining consistency.
Hiding metric definitions defeats discoverability; users who cannot see how a field is defined cannot tell whether it matches their use case or troubleshoot unexpected results.

questions.stem: Which TWO practices strengthen centralized metric governance in Sigma? (Select TWO)

options.text: Define KPI logic once in reusable datasets
options.explanation: Centralizing KPI logic in a single reusable dataset means any update to the definition propagates to all downstream workbooks automatically, preventing metric drift.

options.text: Use certified or endorsed datasets for common reporting
options.explanation: Certification signals that a dataset has been reviewed and approved, making it the authoritative source that users should select for common reporting rather than ad hoc alternatives.

options.text: Allow every workbook author to redefine revenue differently
options.explanation: Allowing each author to independently define revenue produces conflicting numbers across reports, which is the primary problem that centralized governance is designed to solve.

options.text: Remove data dictionary information from the workspace
options.explanation: Removing data dictionary information reduces discoverability and understanding, making it harder for users to find and correctly use the governed definitions.

questions.stem: What problem does a data dictionary solve in a Sigma workspace?

options.text: It explains field meaning and usage so analysts can self-serve more safely
options.explanation: A data dictionary attaches human-readable descriptions to datasets and columns so that analysts can understand what each field means, how it is calculated, and when to use it, reducing errors from misinterpretation.
Credential rotation is an administrative task performed at the connection level in workspace administration, not through the data dictionary.
Slack delivery is a scheduled report or export feature; the data dictionary is metadata documentation, not a delivery mechanism.
Version history tracks edits over time, while the data dictionary documents field semantics; they are independent and complementary features.

questions.stem: Why can long inheritance chains of derived datasets become a maintenance risk?

options.text: Changes upstream may have broad downstream effects that are harder to trace
options.explanation: In a deep inheritance chain, a change to a base dataset's column name, type, or logic can silently break every derived dataset and workbook further down the chain, and tracing the full impact across many levels is time-consuming.

options.text: They prevent all calculations from running
options.explanation: Inheritance chains do not prevent calculations from running; workbooks and derived datasets can still define and execute their own formulas.
Writeback capability is governed by input table configuration and connection permissions, not by the depth of dataset inheritance.

options.text: They force every workbook to be public
options.explanation: Workbook visibility is controlled by workspace folder permissions; a dataset's position in an inheritance chain does not affect whether it or its consumers are public or private.

questions.stem: What is the main purpose of an input table in Sigma?

options.text: To let authorized users enter or update workbook data directly for a controlled use case
options.explanation: Input tables enable controlled writeback scenarios—such as budget entry, plan adjustments, or annotations—where specific users can contribute structured data directly through the Sigma interface, with that data written back to the warehouse.
Input tables complement the warehouse by providing a write path for human-entered data; they do not replace the warehouse as the underlying storage and query engine.
Input tables are a data entry feature; adding one to a workbook has no effect on the formulas or calculations in other elements.
Input tables are data elements used for writeback, not visualization types; chart types are selected separately in workbook elements.

questions.stem: A finance team wants budget owners to enter forecasts beside warehouse actuals in the same workbook. Which Sigma capability best fits?

options.text: Input tables combined with warehouse-backed data in the workbook
options.explanation: Input tables allow budget owners to type forecasts directly into Sigma, which are then written to the warehouse, while adjacent workbook elements query actuals from warehouse tables, enabling a side-by-side view within a single workbook.
A hierarchy defines drill-down paths between dimension levels and does not provide a mechanism for users to enter or persist new data.
A PNG export is a static image of the current workbook state; it does not support data entry or writeback.
SCIM group sync automates user and group provisioning from an identity provider; it manages who can access Sigma but does not enable data entry or forecast capture.

questions.stem: Which type of calculation is best suited to a rolling 12-month average?

options.text: A window-style calculation over an ordered time series
options.explanation: A rolling average requires computing an aggregate over a moving window of preceding and following rows ordered by date, which is exactly what window (analytic) functions are designed to do.
Permission rules control data access for users or groups and are entirely separate from the calculation logic applied to a measure.
A workspace invite manages user onboarding; it has no role in performing analytical computations over a dataset.
A dataset tag is a metadata label used for discovery and governance; it does not perform any calculation on the underlying data.

questions.stem: Why would an analyst use a CASE-style expression in Sigma?

options.text: To implement multi-branch business logic in a calculation
options.explanation: A CASE-style expression evaluates a series of conditions and returns a different value for each matching branch, allowing analysts to encode business rules such as customer tiers, regional groupings, or status categories in a single column.
Credential rotation is an administrative operation performed at the connection level, not a formula type within a workbook calculation.
Template creation is a workbook management action accessed through the workspace interface, not something triggered by a CASE expression.
Dataset certification is an endorsement status set by an admin or designated owner through governance workflows, not a formula result.

questions.stem: A workbook needs year-over-year growth by region while preserving the region partition. What kind of logic is required?

options.text: A time-aware window or period-over-period calculation partitioned by region
options.explanation: Year-over-year growth requires looking up the same metric from the prior year period; in Sigma this is implemented with a period-over-period or LAG-style window function partitioned by region, so each region's growth is computed independently from the correct prior-year baseline.
Embed permission settings govern how embedded workbooks authenticate and what data they expose to external viewers; they do not perform time-series calculations.
A version tag marks a named checkpoint in workbook history for release management; it has no effect on calculation logic.
Warehouse credential rotation is an administrative security task; it does not affect how window functions are defined or computed.

questions.stem: Which TWO functions are most associated with statistical or analytical calculations rather than access control? (Select TWO)

options.explanation: Percentile is a Sigma aggregation function that computes the value at a given rank within a distribution, used for statistical analysis of metric spread.
StdDev computes the standard deviation of a set of values, quantifying variability—a core statistical calculation in analytical workbooks.
SCIM (System for Cross-domain Identity Management) is a protocol for automating user provisioning and deprovisioning; it is an administrative identity feature, not a calculation function.
A webhook subscription is a delivery mechanism that sends workbook output to an external endpoint on a schedule; it is not a calculation applied to data.

questions.stem: What is the main purpose of a cross-element lookup in Sigma?

options.text: To reference a value from another workbook element or related calculation context
options.explanation: A cross-element lookup lets one workbook element—such as a table or chart—reference an aggregated value computed in a different element on the same page, enabling comparisons like "each row vs. the total computed in a summary card."
Warehouse credential management is an administrative function in workspace settings; it is not accessible through a workbook formula.
Dataset certification is a governance status set through endorsement workflows; it is not performed by a lookup expression.
Version history is managed through the workbook's version menu; a cross-element lookup formula has no effect on history.

questions.stem: Why are ordered partitions important for many window functions?

options.text: They define the sequence and grouping over which the analytic result is computed
options.explanation: A window function requires a PARTITION BY clause to define the subset of rows it operates on, and an ORDER BY clause to establish the sequence within that partition; without these, functions like rank, running total, or lag produce undefined or incorrect results.
Workbook edit access is governed by workspace permissions assigned to users or groups, not by partition definitions in a formula.
Publishing a dataset is a workspace action that makes it available for reuse; it has no relationship to the PARTITION BY or ORDER BY syntax in a window function.
Tags are metadata labels applied to datasets or columns through the governance UI, not a product of window function partition logic.

questions.stem: An analyst wants to compare each salesperson against the median performer in the same region. Which approach best fits?

options.text: Use grouped statistical calculations such as percentile or median logic by region
options.explanation: Computing a regional median requires a window aggregate partitioned by region (e.g., Percentile([Sales], 0.5) partitioned by Region), which Sigma's analytic functions support and which produces the correct comparison baseline for each salesperson.
Disabling the semantic layer removes the governed dataset foundation; it does not provide a mechanism for statistical calculations and would make the analysis less reliable, not more.

options.text: Move all data to an input table
options.explanation: Input tables are designed for human data entry and writeback; moving analytics data into an input table is an inappropriate use of the feature and does not enable window calculations.
Dataset descriptions are metadata that help users discover and understand fields; removing them degrades governance without affecting calculation capability.

questions.stem: Why tag a workbook version before a major release?

options.text: It creates a named checkpoint that is easier to review or roll back to later
options.explanation: A named version tag marks a specific point in the workbook's edit history with a human-readable label such as "v1.0-release," making it easy to identify, compare, or restore that exact state if a subsequent release causes problems.
The warehouse engine (Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift, etc.) is determined by the connection configuration in workspace administration; version tagging has no effect on it.
Version tagging is a metadata action that labels a historical snapshot; it does not modify or delete any datasets connected to the workbook.
Sharing permissions are managed through workspace folder and content settings; applying a version tag does not change who can access or collaborate on the workbook.

questions.stem: What is the value of a template workbook in Sigma?

options.text: It standardizes reusable reporting patterns for future work
options.explanation: A template workbook captures an approved layout, set of elements, and formula patterns that authors can copy as a starting point for new workbooks, reducing development time and promoting consistent structure across the organization.
Templates are pre-built workbook structures; they do not replace or affect the datasets they connect to.

options.text: It forces every chart to use the same color
options.explanation: Templates carry whatever formatting was applied when they were saved, but workbook authors can freely change colors and styles after copying; templates do not enforce visual styling.
Governance review is a process decision; using a template does not bypass certification, approval workflows, or permission reviews.

questions.stem: Why review change history after an unexpected workbook regression?

options.text: It helps identify what changed and whether a prior state should be restored
options.explanation: The change history log shows who made each edit and what was modified, allowing the team to pinpoint which change introduced the regression and either revert the workbook to a prior snapshot or surgically fix the specific modification.
Change history is a read-only audit log of edits; it has no capability to repair or rotate warehouse connection credentials.
Change history exposes the full timeline of edits; it makes historical changes visible rather than hiding them.
Workbook certification is an endorsement status granted through governance workflows; reviewing change history is an investigation step and does not automatically promote the workbook's trust level.

questions.stem: What is the main governance benefit of promoting a workbook from draft to published to certified status?

options.text: It signals increasing trust and review maturity to downstream users
options.explanation: The progression from draft to published to certified communicates to consumers that the workbook has undergone increasing levels of review; certified status indicates it has passed quality and governance checks and is appropriate for broad or critical use.

options.text: It converts all formulas to SQL
options.explanation: Sigma workbooks generate SQL queries against the warehouse at runtime regardless of status; the certification state does not change how formulas are evaluated or represented.
Collaboration is managed through permissions; changing a workbook's certification status does not restrict who can view or edit it.
Certification reflects a review process and organizational trust, not a technical guarantee; a certified workbook can still contain errors if the review process did not catch them.

questions.stem: Which TWO actions support reusable development in Sigma? (Select TWO)

options.text: Copying approved elements between workbooks when appropriate
options.explanation: Copying a proven chart, table, or calculation from an existing workbook into a new one leverages already-validated logic, speeding development while maintaining consistency.

options.text: Using template workbooks for standard patterns
options.explanation: Template workbooks encode approved layout and structure, so new workbooks built from the template inherit consistent patterns without starting from scratch.

options.text: Deleting all change history after each publish
options.explanation: Deleting change history removes the ability to audit edits, identify regressions, and roll back to prior states—all of which are essential for maintainable development.
Named version tags before releases are a best practice for release management; skipping them makes rollback and audit much harder after deployment.

questions.stem: What is the difference between Sigma-managed credentials and customer-managed service accounts at a high level?

options.text: They represent different ways the warehouse connection can authenticate
options.explanation: Sigma-managed credentials use Sigma's own service account to authenticate with the warehouse, while customer-managed service accounts allow organizations to supply and control their own warehouse identity, providing more control over audit trails and credential lifecycle.

options.text: One is for charts and the other is for tables only
options.explanation: The credential type applies to the connection as a whole and determines how all queries from that connection authenticate; it is not split by element type.
Credential types are warehouse connection authentication mechanisms, not visualization types.
The choice meaningfully affects who owns the warehouse identity, how permissions are audited, and what happens when credentials need to be rotated.

questions.stem: Why might an organization prefer OAuth or identity-passthrough-style access patterns for some connections?

options.text: They can align warehouse access more closely with the end user identity
options.explanation: OAuth and identity-passthrough patterns forward the individual user's identity to the warehouse, so the warehouse's own row-level security and column masking policies are enforced per user rather than using a shared service account that bypasses those controls.
OAuth passthrough still requires warehouse roles and policies to be designed for each user identity; it does not eliminate the need for permissions architecture.

options.text: They prevent input tables from working
options.explanation: Input table writeback is controlled by the connection's write permission to the target table; it is compatible with OAuth connections that have appropriate write grants.
Cross-connection analysis capabilities are determined by Sigma's feature support and the data model, not by the authentication method used for individual connections.

questions.stem: What is required for Sigma writeback features to persist changes into a warehouse table?

options.text: The connection and target permissions must allow the write operation
options.explanation: Writeback requires the Sigma connection to have INSERT or UPDATE privileges on the target warehouse table; without the appropriate permissions at the connection and target levels, writes will fail regardless of how the input table is configured.

options.text: A map visualization must be present
options.explanation: Map visualizations are geographic chart elements with no dependency relationship to writeback functionality.
Dataset certification is a trust and governance signal; it is not a prerequisite for enabling or executing writeback operations.

options.text: The workbook must be exported first
options.explanation: Writeback operates through the live connection when a user submits changes; exporting the workbook is a separate, unrelated action.

questions.stem: What is the main technical concern with cross-connection joins?

options.text: They may have added complexity or performance considerations because data comes from multiple connections
options.explanation: Joining data from two different warehouse connections requires Sigma to materialize one side of the join before combining it with the other, which can introduce latency, data movement overhead, and increased complexity compared with in-warehouse joins where the engine optimizes execution.
Sigma does support cross-connection analysis in certain configurations; the capability exists but carries the performance and complexity caveats described above.

options.text: They remove the need for datasets
options.explanation: Cross-connection analysis does not eliminate the semantic layer; datasets still govern field definitions and reuse regardless of how many connections contribute data.
SCIM group management is an identity provisioning process triggered by the identity provider, not by how a workbook's data sources are configured.

questions.stem: Which TWO operational tasks matter for healthy warehouse connectivity? (Select TWO)

options.explanation: Service account credentials expire or may be compromised over time; regular rotation and connection health monitoring detect authentication failures before they cause workbook outages.

Warehouses impose compute and storage quotas; workloads that exceed limits can cause query failures, and monitoring consumption trends allows teams to right-size resources or optimize heavy queries proactively.
Removing dataset documentation degrades discoverability and field understanding; it does not improve connection reliability or performance.
Disabling sharing restricts content distribution but has no effect on the underlying connection health or the warehouse's ability to execute queries.

questions.stem: Why certify a dataset in Sigma?

options.text: To indicate it is a trusted, approved source for common analysis
options.explanation: Certification is an endorsement status visible in the data catalog that tells users the dataset has been reviewed, validated, and approved by designated data owners, making it the authoritative source for common reporting.

options.text: To make it impossible to filter
options.explanation: Certification does not restrict any query operations; users can still filter, sort, and aggregate certified datasets exactly as they would uncertified ones.

options.text: To delete the underlying warehouse table
options.explanation: Certification is a metadata status in Sigma; it has no authority over or connection to the warehouse objects that the dataset queries.
Chart type selection is a per-element visualization setting in a workbook; the certification status of a source dataset has no effect on how downstream charts are configured.

questions.stem: What is the main purpose of SCIM provisioning in analytics platforms?

options.text: To automate user and team lifecycle management from an identity provider
options.explanation: SCIM (System for Cross-domain Identity Management) is a standard protocol that enables an identity provider such as Okta or Azure AD to automatically create, update, and deactivate Sigma users and teams in sync with HR or IT system changes, eliminating manual provisioning.
Window functions are analytical calculations defined in workbook formulas; they are not related to user identity management.
Template creation is a workbook management action performed by workspace authors; SCIM manages user accounts, not content.
Warehouse SQL tuning involves query optimization at the database level; SCIM does not interact with the warehouse directly.

questions.stem: Why should embed implementations be governed carefully?

options.text: They can expose analytics to external audiences, so data access boundaries must be deliberate
options.explanation: Embedding a Sigma workbook in an external application or customer portal exposes its data to users who may not have direct Sigma accounts; without deliberate access controls—such as row-level security parameters passed through the embed URL—sensitive data can be over-exposed.

options.text: They convert input tables into charts
options.explanation: Embedding is a delivery mechanism that renders a workbook in an iframe; it does not modify or convert any workbook element types.
The semantic layer (published datasets and their definitions) remains intact when a workbook is embedded; embedding only changes how the workbook is surfaced to end users.

options.text: They remove the need for permissions
options.explanation: Embedding introduces the need for additional permission design, particularly for controlling what data each external user or tenant can see; it does not eliminate the need for permissions.

questions.stem: A workbook is shared widely, but some users should edit input rows while others should only view results. What is the best control principle?

options.text: Separate write permissions from read permissions with least privilege
options.explanation: The principle of least privilege means each user receives only the access level their role requires; editors who need to enter data receive Can Edit access to the input table, while viewers receive View access that allows them to see results but not modify rows.

options.text: Give everyone full edit rights for simplicity
options.explanation: Granting everyone full edit rights removes the distinction between data entry users and read-only consumers, creating risk of accidental or unauthorized data modification.

options.text: Move the workbook to a hidden folder only
options.explanation: Hiding the workbook in a folder restricts who can find it but does not provide the granular per-role access controls needed to distinguish edit from view rights for users who should all have some access.
Disabling the warehouse connection makes the workbook non-functional for everyone, including users who legitimately need to view results.

questions.stem: Which TWO controls commonly contribute to secure sharing in Sigma? (Select TWO)

options.explanation: Workspace and content permissions determine which users can view, edit, or explore specific workbooks and folders in Sigma; without these, any user with a Sigma account could access any content.

Connection-level access controls govern what warehouse data the connection credential can reach; if a shared workbook uses a connection with broad privileges, users may be able to query more data than intended, so connection permissions form a second, complementary control layer.
Chart color settings are purely cosmetic; they have no effect on data security or who can access a workbook.
Dataset tags are metadata for discovery; removing them reduces findability but does not add any security control over data access.

questions.stem: What is a scheduled report primarily used for?

options.text: Delivering workbook results on a schedule through channels such as email or messaging integrations
options.explanation: Scheduled reports automatically deliver a snapshot of a workbook or its elements to specified recipients via email or Slack on a configured cadence, removing the need for recipients to log in to Sigma to get their regular updates.
A dataset inheritance chain is a modeling concept describing how derived datasets build on base datasets; it is not created or managed through the scheduling feature.
Credential rotation is an administrative security task performed in the connections section of workspace administration; it is unrelated to report delivery schedules.
Semantic metrics are defined in the data modeling layer; a scheduled report delivers already-defined results, it does not create new metric definitions.

questions.stem: Why might a team use a webhook delivery for workbook outputs?

options.text: To integrate report delivery with an external workflow or system
options.explanation: Webhook delivery sends a structured payload containing workbook data or a notification to an external endpoint (such as a Slack app, Zapier workflow, or internal microservice) on a schedule or trigger, enabling downstream automation without requiring the recipient to access Sigma directly.
Webhook delivery is an output/notification mechanism; it does not interact with or replace the warehouse query engine that powers workbook calculations.
Enabling webhooks adds a delivery channel; it does not disable other export options such as CSV, PDF, or email.

Input-table permissions are governed by workspace access settings applied to the workbook; webhook configuration affects how data is delivered externally and has no effect on who can write to input tables.

questions.stem: What is the main difference between sharing a workbook and certifying a dataset?

options.text: Sharing controls distribution of content, while certification signals trust in a data asset
options.explanation: Sharing a workbook is a permission action that grants named users or teams access to view or edit that content, while certifying a dataset is a governance action that attaches an endorsement badge indicating the dataset has been validated as a trusted source.
The two actions operate on different objects (workbooks vs. datasets), through different UI surfaces, and produce different effects; they are not equivalent.
Certification adds an endorsement status visible in the catalog; it does not alter any visual formatting within a workbook.
Sharing affects access to the workbook as a whole—views, tables, charts, and all—not just its formula definitions.

questions.stem: A company wants to expose a customer-facing embedded workbook but only with a narrow, approved slice of data. What matters most?

options.text: Design the embedding and underlying permissions so exposed data is intentionally limited
options.explanation: In a customer-facing embed the primary risk is over-exposing data; the embed URL should pass user-level parameters (such as a customer_id claim) that drive row-level security filters, and the connection credential must have the minimum warehouse privileges needed for the approved data slice.

options.text: Increase the number of workbook tabs
options.explanation: Adding workbook tabs increases the surface area of the embed rather than restricting it; more tabs typically means more data exposed, not less.
Dataset descriptions are metadata annotations; removing them degrades internal discoverability but has no effect on what data the embedded workbook exposes to customers.
Version tags are release management checkpoints; disabling them removes rollback capability but does not restrict what data the embed surfaces.

questions.stem: Which TWO statements about governed sharing are most accurate? (Select TWO)

options.text: Effective sharing depends on both the workbook permissions and underlying data access
options.explanation: A user who has workbook view permission but lacks the underlying connection or dataset access will encounter permission errors or see filtered data; effective access requires alignment between content permissions and data-layer grants.

options.text: Certified datasets help consumers trust reused content
options.explanation: The certified badge visible in the Sigma catalog signals that an owner has reviewed and endorsed the dataset, giving consumers confidence that the metric definitions are accurate and appropriate for reporting.

options.text: Slack delivery automatically grants data-source access to everyone
options.explanation: Slack delivery sends a snapshot or link to the designated recipients; it does not grant any new warehouse or Sigma permissions to the Slack workspace or its members.

options.text: Scheduled exports remove the need for access review
options.explanation: Scheduled exports automate delivery of output; they do not replace the need to periodically review whether recipients still require access or whether the shared content contains data that should no longer be distributed.

questions.stem: Why might an operations team use an input table for annotations?

options.text: To capture human notes or adjustments directly in the analytic workflow
options.explanation: Input tables allow team members to type contextual notes—such as reasons for a spike, known data quality issues, or manual overrides—directly alongside the warehouse data in the same workbook, making annotations part of the persisted analytic record.

options.text: To replace all warehouse fact tables
options.explanation: Input tables are designed for small volumes of human-entered data; they are not a substitute for the warehouse fact tables that store operational transaction data at scale.

options.text: To stop users from filtering data
options.explanation: Filtering is a workbook interaction controlled by filter elements and control settings; input tables do not restrict or enable filtering.
Version history is a workbook feature managed independently of the data elements within it; adding or removing an input table does not affect version tracking.

questions.stem: What is the main purpose of a correlation-style statistic in an analytics workbook?

options.text: To estimate the strength of relationship between variables
options.explanation: A correlation statistic (such as Pearson's r) measures the linear association between two numeric variables, producing a coefficient between -1 and 1 that quantifies how strongly and in what direction the variables move together.
Permission assignment is an administrative action performed through workspace settings or folder sharing dialogs; it is not a statistical calculation.

options.text: To publish a report on a schedule
options.explanation: Scheduled report publication is a delivery feature configured in the workbook scheduling settings; it does not involve any statistical computation.
SCIM configuration is an identity management integration set up in workspace administration; it has nothing to do with statistical analysis of data.

questions.stem: Why should release managers avoid publishing major workbook changes without a named version or checkpoint?

options.text: Rollback and audit become harder if the release causes problems
options.explanation: Without a named version tag before a major change, there is no labeled rollback point; if the new version introduces a regression, the team must manually review unlabeled auto-save entries in the history to find the pre-release state, which is error-prone and time-consuming.

options.text: The workbook can no longer connect to data
options.explanation: A workbook's connection to the warehouse is determined by the data source configuration and credentials; it is not affected by whether a version tag was created.
Input table write behavior is controlled by connection permissions and workbook settings; the absence of a version tag does not interrupt data entry.
SCIM provisioning is triggered by the identity provider's sync schedule and events; it operates independently of workbook version management.

questions.stem: What is connection health monitoring intended to catch?

options.text: Issues such as broken credentials, unavailable backends, or operational problems with a connection
options.explanation: Connection health monitoring checks whether the warehouse endpoint is reachable, the service account credentials are valid, and queries can be submitted successfully; it surfaces authentication failures, network timeouts, and quota exhaustion before they silently break workbooks.
Chart color configuration is a visual design setting in a workbook element; it is not within the scope of connection health monitoring.

options.text: Whether a workbook has enough labels
options.explanation: Label density in a workbook is a visualization quality concern, not a signal that connection health monitoring tracks.
Dataset certification status is a governance attribute managed separately; connection monitoring focuses on infrastructure availability and authentication, not on content trust levels.

questions.stem: Why is it risky to assume workspace permissions alone are enough to secure data?

options.text: Underlying connection and warehouse access also influence what data can actually be queried
options.explanation: Sigma workspace permissions control who can open and interact with a workbook, but the underlying connection credential determines what warehouse data those queries can reach; if the connection uses a broad service account, users with view-only workspace access may still indirectly access sensitive tables that the workbook queries.

options.text: Workspace permissions override every other control automatically
options.explanation: Workspace permissions are additive and can be overridden by more restrictive warehouse-level controls; they do not supersede database roles or row-level security policies.
Certified datasets carry an endorsement status that indicates trust; they remain subject to the same permission checks as uncertified datasets and do not bypass access controls.
SCIM automates user lifecycle management (provisioning and deprovisioning); it does not eliminate the need for carefully designed data access permissions.

questions.stem: What is the most likely reason to send scheduled workbook results to Slack or email?

options.text: To distribute timely insights to stakeholders without requiring them to open the workbook each time
options.explanation: Scheduled delivery pushes a fresh snapshot of key metrics directly into stakeholders' existing communication channels, reducing friction for regular consumers who do not need or want to log in to Sigma to get their routine updates.
Rewriting the semantic layer involves modifying dataset definitions and field logic; it is a data modeling action completely separate from scheduled delivery.
A cross-connection join is a data preparation technique that combines sources from multiple connections; it is configured in the workbook's data source settings, not through a delivery schedule.
Version tags are applied through the workbook's version history menu as a release management action; they are not created by or related to scheduled report delivery.

questions.stem: Which TWO design choices usually improve self-service while preserving governance in Sigma? (Select TWO)

options.text: Provide trusted published datasets with clear metric definitions
options.explanation: Published datasets with pre-defined, approved metric logic give analysts a reliable starting point that reflects business definitions, enabling exploration without risking metric inconsistency.

options.text: Add descriptions and tags so fields are understandable
options.explanation: Field descriptions explain what each column measures and how it is calculated, while tags make fields searchable; together they reduce the risk that analysts will misinterpret or misuse fields they encounter during self-service exploration.

options.text: Force every user to query raw warehouse tables directly
options.explanation: Bypassing the semantic layer and querying raw tables directly exposes analysts to undefined, inconsistent source fields and removes the governance guardrails that centralized dataset definitions provide.
Hiding certification status removes the most visible signal that distinguishes trusted, reviewed datasets from experimental ones, making it harder for consumers to choose the right source.

questions.stem: An analyst wants week-over-week comparison for each product family. What additional modeling detail matters most?

options.text: The calculation needs the correct time ordering and product partitioning
options.explanation: A week-over-week comparison uses a LAG or DateDiff-style window calculation; the PARTITION BY product family clause ensures each family's prior week is looked up independently, and the ORDER BY date clause ensures weeks are compared in the correct sequence.

options.text: The workbook must be certified first
options.explanation: Certification is a governance endorsement applied after the workbook is built and validated; it is not a prerequisite for defining or running calculations.
Cross-cloud connectivity is a warehouse architecture consideration unrelated to the formula logic needed for a period-over-period calculation.
Embedding is a distribution method; the calculation logic for week-over-week comparison is identical whether the workbook is embedded or accessed directly in Sigma.

questions.stem: What is the main purpose of approval workflows around certification or endorsement?

options.text: To signal that a dataset has been reviewed before broad reuse
options.explanation: An approval workflow for certification requires that a designated data owner or governance team review the dataset's definitions, lineage, and accuracy before the certification badge is applied, ensuring the endorsement reflects genuine validation rather than self-certification.

options.text: To turn all metrics into writeback tables
options.explanation: Certification is a trust signal, not a data type conversion; it does not alter the structure or storage mechanism of the dataset's underlying metrics.
Workbook commenting is a collaboration feature controlled independently of certification workflows; certification approval does not affect comment visibility or availability.
Workbook history is an edit audit trail maintained by the platform; running a certification workflow does not delete or hide prior revisions.
